title: VMSS without OS auto-upgrade + public SSH
narrative: A VM Scale Set has OS auto-upgrade disabled and its instance count exposes SSH to the internet. Instances run months-old kernels; any published CVE with public exploit gives one-click RCE on every instance in the pool.

steps.action: Match VMSS banner to known kernel CVE.
Exploit kernel CVE; root shell on all instances.

title: Container App with external ingress + managed identity overprivileged
narrative: A Container App accepts public HTTP ingress AND its user-assigned managed identity holds Contributor at subscription scope. Any RCE in the app yields Azure management-plane takeover.

steps.action: Exploit app-layer vuln (SSRF, deserialization) in the Container App.
Call IMDS; get ARM token scoped as Contributor.

title: App Service without auth + connection string in app settings
narrative: An App Service has Easy Auth disabled and its app settings store database + storage connection strings. An attacker hitting the unauthenticated /api routes can often trigger endpoints that inadvertently expose environment variables (error pages, debug endpoints).

steps.action: Probe the unauth API for a stack trace or /env endpoint.
Extract connection strings; connect directly to backends.

title: Function App with over-privileged Graph permission
narrative: A Function App is granted Directory.ReadWrite.All via its managed identity — the developer couldn't figure out the least-privilege scope. Any prompt-injection / template-injection / upstream-library-compromise against the function gives attackers tenant-wide directory write.

steps.action: Exploit injection vuln in function HTTP handler.
Use MI's Directory.ReadWrite.All to create admin users.

title: AKS private cluster with unrestricted egress
narrative: AKS is private (no public API server) but the cluster's egress firewall allows any outbound destination. A compromised pod can exfiltrate or call out to C2 freely, and the 'private cluster' label gives the security team a false sense of containment.

steps.action: Establish outbound C2 tunnel through unrestricted NAT.
Exfil cluster secrets, node identity tokens.

title: AKS node pool SSH enabled
narrative: AKS node pool VMs have SSH enabled and accept password auth. SSH is meant for on-call debugging; the node has full kubelet credentials and can impersonate the cluster to the control plane.

steps.action: Brute-force SSH against node public IP or peered subnet.
Read /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf; impersonate kubelet.

title: AKS without network policies — pod-to-pod free-for-all
narrative: AKS cluster has no NetworkPolicy / Calico enforcement. Any compromised pod can freely reach other pods' services — including kube-system, metrics servers, and the metadata proxy. There is no lateral segmentation inside the cluster.

steps.action: Scan cluster DNS for service endpoints.
Exploit an in-cluster unauthenticated service.
steps.gain: Second pod compromise via cluster-internal reachability.

title: AKS privileged pod + host path mount
narrative: A pod runs privileged: true AND mounts /var/run/docker.sock or /host. Container escape is trivial — the pod can exec into the host, ptrace other containers, or create new containers with arbitrary parameters.

steps.action: Access the host socket; docker run --privileged -v /:/host attacker.
Dump kubelet creds; cluster admin.
steps.gain: Cluster admin from single pod compromise.

title: AKS image pulled from untrusted registry
narrative: Pods pull images from public Docker Hub without image signing / admission control. A supply-chain compromise (typosquat, namespace takeover) yields attacker-controlled code in every pod restart.

steps.action: Publish malicious image to a registry path similar to the real one.
On pod restart pulls the malicious image; attacker code runs in-cluster.

blast_radius.max_privilege: Pod-level RCE; often cluster-admin via privileged container.

title: Service Fabric cluster with TLS downgrade
narrative: A Service Fabric cluster accepts TLS 1.0 for node-to-node communication. A legacy-protocol downgrade attack on the management endpoint yields cluster admin — Service Fabric node identity is the root of trust for the entire cluster.

steps.action: Force TLS 1.0 negotiation; MITM the session.
Replay node auth; join attacker node as cluster member.
steps.gain: Service Fabric node that controls the cluster.

title: Batch account compute pool with storage cred in init script
narrative: Azure Batch pool startup task contains a plaintext storage account key. Every pool VM boots with this in its metadata. An attacker on any Batch node can read it and access the storage account from elsewhere.

steps.action: Read CustomData / startup script.
Authenticate from outside the Batch network.

services_at_risk: Any app sharing the storage account

title: App Configuration exposed without private endpoint
narrative: Azure App Configuration is reachable publicly and some keys carry feature-flag defaults that leak roadmap info, along with secret references that can be resolved to Key Vault URLs. A token with App Config Reader reveals more than intended.

steps.action: List app config entries.
Resolve secret references; extract referenced Key Vault secrets.
steps.gain: Secret values beyond what App Config holds directly.

title: VMSS custom image drift — older CVE-prone version
narrative: VMSS uses a custom image built 18 months ago. New instances boot with the same stale OS and package versions. The fleet drifts from 'golden image' security posture over time, silently accumulating exploitable CVEs.

steps.action: CVE-2024-xxxx published; image is affected.
Attack the fleet; every instance vulnerable.

title: Container Registry with admin user enabled
narrative: ACR has admin user enabled — a single shared credential that nobody rotates. CI systems and developers all use the same username/password. A single leak yields push rights into the registry, so attackers can insert malicious images.

steps.action: Harvest the ACR admin password from a CI log or developer machine.
Push a trojaned 'latest' tag; wait for pods to pull.

blast_radius.max_privilege: Push access to every repo in ACR.
services_at_risk: Every service pulling from this ACR

title: ACR anonymous pull + AKS no image policy
narrative: ACR allows anonymous pull and AKS has no admission control on image source. An attacker registered in ACR as a pusher (via any other chain) can publish images the cluster will pull blindly.

steps.action: Push attacker-image to a commonly-pulled repo path.
Anonymous-pull the image; no signature check.

title: AKS ingress controller without WAF
narrative: AKS nginx-ingress is exposed to the internet without an AKS-attached WAF or Front Door in front. SQL injection, XSS, and RCE attempts hit backends directly. Traffic logging goes to ingress pod stdout only.

steps.action: Send OWASP Top-10 payloads; no WAF filters them.
Exploit reaches backend service.

title: AKS cluster autoscaler unrestricted — DoS via pod spam
narrative: Cluster autoscaler has no upper node-count limit and pod QoS has no ResourceQuota. A tenant-scoped attacker creates thousands of pending pods; cluster scales out to absorb them, generating a huge Azure bill.

steps.action: kubectl create -f thousand-pods.yaml.
Sustain load; financial damage + noisy-neighbor DoS.

title: Spot VM + unhandled eviction exposes running data
narrative: Spot VMs are evicted with 30-second notice; apps that don't checkpoint leave in-memory data in page files on the VHD. If disk encryption isn't enforced for ephemeral OS disks, a later attacker-controlled Spot allocation reuses the same hardware and can potentially recover residual data.

steps.action: Evict spot VM; data written to disk not fully wiped.
Lucky-allocation yields the same underlying storage; extract residual data.

title: Windows VM with Legacy Auth / NTLM enabled
narrative: A Windows VM accepts NTLM authentication and exposes SMB (445) to the VNet. An attacker who compromises a low-privilege VM can SMB-relay against the target, capturing a domain-hashed credential for offline crack.

steps.action: Run ntlmrelayx against target VM.
Crack offline; pass the hash.

title: Container App Environment shared across dev/prod
narrative: Dev and prod Container Apps share a single Managed Environment. Dev teams push changes without staging; a vulnerable dev app in the same VNet can reach prod services and secrets over the shared internal network.

steps.action: Compromise dev Container App (weaker controls).
Pivot to prod services over the shared VNet.

title: VM extension custom script contains embedded password
narrative: A VM is provisioned with a custom-script extension that passes credentials as script arguments. Anyone with Reader on the VM can view the extension's settings including plaintext args. No secret rotation, no history.

steps.action: View VM extension settings via ARM API.
Use credential to authenticate elsewhere.

title: VM Monitor agent missing — no host forensics
narrative: Production VMs don't have Azure Monitor Agent installed. During IR, the SOC has neither process execution logs nor file-integrity events. Forensics depends entirely on host-level artifacts captured post-incident.

steps.action: Compromise VM; actions leave no host-level telemetry streamed out.
Cannot reconstruct attacker actions after the fact.

title: Container App secret rotation miss
narrative: Container App secrets (API keys, DB passwords) are set at deploy time and never rotated. A developer leaving the team still has access because the secret they provisioned is still live.

steps.action: Use the unchanged secret months later.
Pivot to downstream systems.

title: Kubernetes workload identity shared across namespaces
narrative: Azure Workload Identity federation maps one Entra identity to a service account referenced in both dev and prod namespaces. A dev-namespace RCE yields the identity's tokens — same tokens prod uses. Namespace boundary is meaningless.

steps.action: Federated token exchange for the shared Entra ID.
Use identity to access resources intended only for prod pods.

services_at_risk: Any Azure resource granted to the shared ID

title: VM disk encryption key rotation missed
narrative: VMs use Azure Disk Encryption but the encryption key has not been rotated in 3+ years. A former Key Vault admin with a copy of the key can still decrypt any disk snapshot made during that period.

steps.action: Obtain an old snapshot (backup archive, cold storage).
Decrypt using retained key.

services_at_risk: Any VM disk from the key's lifetime
